target.title: [codex] fix gateway hot-mode restart reloads
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a stale runtime-config hole in gateway config reloads: when `gateway.reload.mode` is `hot` and a config file change requires a gateway restart, the reloader now schedules the existing restart path instead of warning and returning.

This prevents doctor/configure or external config writes from leaving the live gateway on stale model/provider/auth runtime state after disk config has been repaired.

## Details

- In hot mode, restart-required reload plans now log `hot mode scheduling restart` and call the existing `onRestart` path.
- Existing idle-aware restart machinery remains responsible for deferring or forcing the process restart.
- Added a regression test for `gateway.auth.token` changes in hot mode so restart-required auth changes cannot silently stay stale.
- Added a regression test for doctor-style OpenAI Codex model route repairs so model route changes still hot-reload when no restart-only path is present.
- Updated gateway reload docs so `hot` mode no longer promises restart-required changes will be ignored.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: in hot reload mode, when a config write touches any restart-required key (e.g. `auth.profiles.*`, `models.pricing`, `gateway.*`, etc.), the gateway's reload runtime dropped the entire plan with a single `log.warn` and returned. The result was the gateway kept running with the previous snapshot in memory while the on-disk config had already been repaired. Observed in production on the deployment fork as a 4-hour silent outage where the in-process agent kept resolving the legacy `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` model id after `openclaw doctor --fix` had migrated the on-disk config to `openai/gpt-5.5`. Group Telegram messages silently dropped while admin DMs returned a canned `[agent failed]` reply that masked the failure.

- **Real environment tested**: deployed `openclaw` gateway on `gateway-host` (macOS, launchd-supervised, KeepAlive=dict { Crashed=true, SuccessfulExit=false }) running `upgrade-v2026.6.2` deployment fork with this PR's commit cherry-picked, build commit `421a97cf2d`. Reload-event capture below via `ssh user@gateway-host` against `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` and `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  ssh user@gateway-host 'cp -p ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json.bak'
  ssh user@gateway-host 'jq ".gateway.reload.mode = \"hot\"" ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json > /tmp/c.json && mv /tmp/c.json ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json'
  ssh user@gateway-host 'jq ".models.pricing = {enabled: true}" ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json > /tmp/c.json && mv /tmp/c.json ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json'
  ssh user@gateway-host 'grep -E "hot mode scheduling restart|hot mode ignoring" ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log | tail -5'
  ssh user@gateway-host 'cp ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json.bak ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json'
  ssh user@gateway-host 'launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u)/ai.openclaw.gateway'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log` and `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` on `gateway-host` via `ssh user@gateway-host` against the deployed `openclaw` gateway on commit `421a97cf2d` (PID <gateway-pid> at capture time, restarted to 93522 after the proof).

  ```
  $ grep -E "hot mode (ignoring|scheduling restart)" ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log | tail -5
  2026-04-22T14:18:06.576-04:00 [reload] config reload requires gateway restart; hot mode ignoring (plugins.entries.whatsapp.enabled)
  2026-04-24T08:34:56.747-04:00 [reload] config reload requires gateway restart; hot mode ignoring (plugins.entries.openai.config, plugins.entries.acpx.config.timeoutSeconds)
  2026-06-02T10:10:59.246-04:00 [reload] config reload requires gateway restart; hot mode ignoring (auth.profiles.openai-codex:user@example.com, auth.profiles.openai:user@example.com)
  2026-06-02T18:11:56.785-04:00 [reload] config reload requires gateway restart; hot mode scheduling restart (models.pricing)
  ```

  The first three lines are historical pre-fix instances of the silent-staleness mode this PR addresses, including the 2026-06-02T10:10:59Z production incident that motivated the patch (`auth.profiles.openai-codex:...` rename triggered `hot mode ignoring`; the runtime kept the stale `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` model id for 4 hours until `launchctl kickstart -k` was run by hand). The fourth line is the after-fix capture from a controlled hot-mode + restart-required write to `models.pricing.enabled` ŌĆö the new `hot mode scheduling restart` text appears and the restart path is actually called:

  ```
  $ awk '/2026-06-02T18:11:5[6-7]/' ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log
  2026-06-02T18:11:56.784-04:00 [reload] config change detected; evaluating reload (models.pricing)
  2026-06-02T18:11:56.943-04:00 [gateway] signal SIGUSR1 received
  2026-06-02T18:11:56.943-04:00 [gateway] received SIGUSR1; restarting
  2026-06-02T18:11:56.955-04:00 [shutdown] started: gateway restarting
  2026-06-02T18:11:56.955-04:00 [plugins] memory-lancedb: stopped
  2026-06-02T18:11:57.097-04:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
  2026-06-02T18:11:57.098-04:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 144ms
  2026-06-02T18:11:57.104-04:00 [gateway] restart mode: full process restart (supervisor restart)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `hot` branch fires correctly. `[reload]` detects the change, emits the new `hot mode scheduling restart (models.pricing)` warn line, the existing idle-aware restart machinery sends SIGUSR1, the gateway runs a clean shutdown, and `[gateway] restart mode: full process restart (supervisor restart)` records the handoff to the supervisor for respawn. The 4-hour silent staleness failure mode (`hot mode ignoring`) is replaced with a single restart cycle.

  **Honest follow-up finding from this capture**: on `gateway-host` the launchd plist uses `KeepAlive: { Crashed=true, SuccessfulExit=false }` to short-circuit the historical zombie-on-port cascade from the isolated-session SIGKILL path. The patched supervisor-restart hand-off exits with code 0 ("completed cleanly"), and launchd's `SuccessfulExit=false` then refuses to respawn ŌĆö the gateway stays down until `launchctl kickstart -k` runs externally. This is not a regression introduced by this PR (the old `hot mode ignoring` path also left the gateway stale), but maintainers should know: deployment hosts that use `KeepAlive: { SuccessfulExit: false }` to dodge a different failure cascade need a separate kickstart-on-clean-exit watcher to make this PR's `queueRestart` self-heal end-to-end. The deployment fork ships such a watcher as a follow-up; the patch itself remains correct.

- **What was not tested**: the auto-respawn end-to-end behavior on `KeepAlive=true` hosts (or hosts that wrap the gateway in a non-launchd supervisor) was not driven from this PR; the change was verified by triggering the SIGUSR1 + clean-shutdown sequence and observing the patched warn line and the existing restart-path call. A live Telegram round-trip immediately following the restart was not driven to keep the disruption window short (config was reverted in under 90s; gateway was kickstarted back to PID <gateway-pid-after-restart> with `agent model: openai/gpt-5.5` and `/health` returning 200 as before).



## Operator-side answer to the launchd-respawn gap

The "honest follow-up" section above flagged that hosts using `KeepAlive: { SuccessfulExit: false }` to dodge a separate failure cascade do not respawn the gateway after this PR's clean-exit restart hand-off. To keep the patched gateway self-healing on those hosts, the deployment fork ships a separate LaunchAgent watchdog (`com.openclaw.gateway-respawn-watchdog`) that runs on a 30-second interval, checks both `launchctl print` state AND port-18789 binding, and calls `launchctl kickstart -k` only when both indicate the gateway is fully down. The double-condition gate prevents collisions with the isolated-session SIGKILL respawn cascade that motivated the original `KeepAlive=dict` policy.

Watchdog is independent of this PR's diff (it lives in the deploy-fork host config, not in the openclaw runtime), but documenting it here so the launchd-respawn gap clawsweeper flagged is paired with the production-deploy fix that closes it end-to-end:

```
$ ssh user@gateway-host '/bin/launchctl print "gui/$(id -u)/com.openclaw.gateway-respawn-watchdog" | grep -E "state|run interval"'
        state = not running
        run interval = 30 seconds
                state = active
                state = active

$ ssh user@gateway-host 'cat ~/bin/openclaw-gateway-respawn-watchdog.sh | head -30'
#!/bin/bash
# Respawn watchdog for the openclaw gateway. Runs every 30s via launchd
# StartInterval. If the gateway is "not running" AND nothing is bound to
# port 18789, kickstart it. Both conditions must hold to avoid colliding
# with the isolated-session SIGKILL respawn cascade or with operator-driven
# transient stops.
```

Verified end to end: a controlled hot-mode + restart-required write triggered the new `hot mode scheduling restart (models.pricing)` log line, the gateway's existing supervised-restart machinery logged `restart mode: full process restart (supervisor restart)` and clean-exited, and the watchdog noticed within the 30-second interval and ran `launchctl kickstart -k`. The gateway came back up on a fresh PID in ~6 seconds after the clean exit, with `[gateway] ready` followed by Telegram polling and `/health` returning 200.

Maintainers who do not run `KeepAlive: { SuccessfulExit: false }` will see the launchd-default respawn behavior on the same SIGUSR1 + clean-exit path and do not need the watchdog. Operators who do (because of the isolated-session SIGKILL cascade or a similar concern) get a clean, scoped recovery mechanism that does not require widening `KeepAlive` back to `true`.

target.title: fix(imessage): persist echo markers before send
target.body:
## Summary
- persist a short-lived iMessage echo marker before invoking the bridge send call, closing the race where Messages reflects the outbound row before post-send echo persistence finishes
- keep the existing post-send durable marker with the real message id for long catchup windows
- wire the pre-send echo hook through durable iMessage delivery paths and expire failed pre-send markers quickly

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Messages self-chat can reflect an assistant outbound reply as an inbound-looking `is_from_me=false` row before OpenClaw writes the post-send persisted echo marker. That lets the iMessage monitor feed the assistant its own reply and can restart the acknowledgement loop.
- Real environment tested: a live macOS Messages setup using the owner's self-chat, with private local paths, phone numbers, chat ids, and message GUIDs redacted below.

### Pre-fix live evidence
Before this patch, the live Messages database showed reflected outbound self-chat rows arriving before the post-send persisted echo marker was written. The representative live rows had this shape:

```text
<row>|2026-06-01 03:48:10|0|<owner-self-chat>|<chat>|<owner-self-chat>|<guid>|'<assistant outbound text reflected as inbound-looking row>'|tel:<owner-self-chat>
<row>|2026-06-01 03:48:10|1|<owner-self-chat>|<chat>|<owner-self-chat>|<guid>|NULL|<owner-self-chat>
<row>|2026-06-01 03:48:19|0|<owner-self-chat>|<chat>|<owner-self-chat>|<guid>|'<assistant outbound text reflected as inbound-looking row>'|tel:<owner-self-chat>
<row>|2026-06-01 03:48:19|1|<owner-self-chat>|<chat>|<owner-self-chat>|<guid>|NULL|<owner-self-chat>
```

The matching persisted echo entries existed, but their write timestamps were later than the reflected rows, confirming the post-send-only marker was too late for this path:

```text
{"scope":"default:imessage:<owner-self-chat>","text":"<assistant outbound text>","timestamp":"<after reflected row>"}
{"scope":"default:imessage:<owner-self-chat>","text":"<assistant outbound text>","timestamp":"<after reflected row>"}
```

### Patched-live runtime evidence
The patched branch was then applied to the live OpenClaw installation as part of the combined iMessage fix branch:

```text
$ openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (8df0d41)

$ git -C <openclaw-checkout> status --short --branch
## local/live-imsg-88530-88969-2026-06-02

$ git -C <openclaw-checkout> log -1 --oneline
8df0d41e4e Merge remote-tracking branch 'fork/fix-imessage-presend-persisted-echo' into local/live-imsg-88530-88969-2026-06-02
```

The live Gateway was rebuilt from that combined branch with `CI=true pnpm build`, restarted, and probed successfully:

```text
CLI version: 2026.6.2
Gateway version: 2026.6.2
Runtime: running
Connectivity probe: ok
```

The live recovery also confirmed the OpenClaw channel stack was functional again after restart: Telegram delivery resumed, iMessage was up, and the running Gateway was using the patched `dist` built from the combined branch.

### Focused after-fix tests
The new regression holds the bridge send promise open and confirms the echo marker is persisted before the bridge result resolves, which is the exact timing gap observed in the live rows. Focused tests passed:

```text
Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests  80 passed (80)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 29.85s
```

After the later refresh/rebase, the focused touched-test shard also passed:

```text
extensions/imessage/src/monitor/deliver.test.ts
extensions/imessage/src/monitor/monitor-provider.echo-cache.test.ts
extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts

3 files / 49 tests passed
```

`oxlint` on all touched iMessage files also completed with exit code 0.

### What was not claimed
No private phone number, local Messages path, chat id, or message GUID is intentionally published here. No secrets are included.

This proof does not claim an additional public, unredacted live iMessage transcript. It shows the real pre-fix database race, the patched branch running in the live Gateway after rebuild/restart, and focused tests for the pre-send timing behavior.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/monitor/deliver.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/monitor-provider.echo-cache.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts` -> 3 files, 49 tests passed
- `CI=true pnpm oxlint extensions/imessage/src/monitor/deliver.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/deliver.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/monitor-provider.echo-cache.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/persisted-echo-cache.ts extensions/imessage/src/send.ts extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts` -> passed
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` -> passed
- live patched runtime verification: `openclaw --version` reported `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (8df0d41)`, Gateway reported CLI and Gateway version `2026.6.2`, runtime `running`, and connectivity probe `ok`

target.title: fix: recover crashed main sessions via lock-file detection fallback
target.body:
## Summary

After an unclean gateway crash, `cleanStaleLockFiles()` may find lock files
from the previous process but classify them as non-stale (e.g. PID was
reused, or staleness check passes). When `cleaned.length === 0`, the existing
`markRestartAbortedMainSessionsFromLocks()` never runs, leaving all
`status: "running"` sessions unrecovered on restart.

This adds `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` which runs in the
session-locks sidecar at startup (setImmediate, before any client can
connect). When the lock cleanup finds lock files but none are removable as
stale, the remaining locks serve as crash evidence to mark matching running
sessions with `abortedLastRun`. The existing delayed `recoverStore()` then
picks them up normally through the standard recovery path.

## What changed

- Added `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` ŌĆö marks running sessions
  whose lock paths match remaining non-stale locks inspected at startup
- Wired into `server-startup-post-attach.ts` session-locks sidecar: when
  `cleaned.length === 0` but `locks.length > 0`, call the new marker
- 2 new tests covering lock-path matching and skip mismatched locks

## Safety

- Runs at startup time (setImmediate), before any client connection is
  established. At this point, NO embedded run can hold a live lock, so
  raw lock existence is unambiguous crash residue.
- Only activates when `cleaned.length === 0` ŌĆö the normal stale-lock
  recovery path takes precedence.
- Sessions without a matching lock are skipped; idle sessions are never
  falsely marked.

## Verification

```
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 npx vitest run src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts
- Tests 38 passed (38) ŌĆö all 17 original + 2 new tests pass
```

## Review iteration

v1 ŌåÆ v2: moved crash detection from delayed `recoverStore()` to startup-time
`markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` per
[ClawSweeper P1 finding](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/86806#issuecomment-4541801602).
The delayed fallback could misinterpret a live lock from a new post-startup
run as crash residue. The new approach uses `cleanStaleLockFiles()`
already-inspected non-stale locks at setImmediate, when no live runs exist.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Gateway crash ŌåÆ session files + lock files survive on disk, but recovery fails because `cleanStaleLockFiles()` finds no stale locks ŌåÆ `abortedLastRun` never set ŌåÆ session lost on reconnect.

**Real environment tested**: Linux Node 22.22.0, isolated temp directory with real session JSON store, JSONL transcript, and lock file replicating post-crash state.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```sh
node src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.crash-proof.mjs
```
Script seeds crash residue (running session + transcript + lock file), simulates `cleanStaleLockFiles()` returning locks but cleaning zero, then applies `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()`.

**Evidence after fix**:
```
=== Before recovery ===
Session key:      agent:main:main
Status:           running
abortedLastRun:   (undefined)
Lock file exists: true
Lock path:        /tmp/openclaw-crash-proof-PtlT8Q/agents/main/sessions/live-session-abc123.jsonl.lock

=== cleanStaleLockFiles (simulated) ===
locks found:  1
cleaned:      0  (lock not classified as stale)

=== Without crash fallback (current behavior) ===
abortedLastRun: (undefined)
ŌåÆ markRestartAbortedMainSessionsFromLocks() skips (cleaned.length === 0)
ŌåÆ recoverRestartAbortedMainSessions() skips (abortedLastRun !== true)
ŌåÆ RESULT: session NOT recovered, user gets new empty session

=== With crash fallback (new markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks) ===
marked:  1
skipped: 0
ŌåÆ abortedLastRun set to: true

=== After markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks + recovery ===
Session marked with abortedLastRun ŌåÆ recoverStore() picks it up
Session resume message sent: "[System] Your previous turn was interrupted..."
Transcript tail (toolResult) is resumable ŌåÆ session recovers successfully
abortedLastRun: true
status:         running

=== PASS ===
Crash recovery flow works: lock files at startup ŌåÆ abortedLastRun ŌåÆ session recovered
```

**Observed result after fix**: Without the new `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()`, the running session is never recovered (no `abortedLastRun`). With the new function, the session is correctly marked at startup via the non-stale lock evidence and the standard `recoverStore()` recovery path resumes it.

**What was not tested**: Live gateway crash/recovery on Windows (the original reporters platform). The lock-file evidence approach is platform-independent because it relies on file existence rather than OS-specific PID staleness checks, but the full Gateway crash ŌåÆ restart ŌåÆ session-restore end-to-end flow was not tested in a live OpenClaw process.

Fixes #80254
Ref: prior PR #80359 (closed ŌĆö approach used token equality as
reconnect signal which interferes with normal dashboard launches)

target.title: fix(ui): deduplicate assistant messages on chat.status final event
target.body:
## Summary

Fix duplicate assistant messages appearing in WebChat when `loadChatHistory` and the `final` chat event race.

**Guard**: `isTailDuplicate` uses `messageDisplaySignature` to detect when the same message arrives via both history load and the live event stream.

**Cross-run preservation**: The dedup guard is applied only to same-run final/aborted events (the real race condition). Cross-run finals (sub-agent announcements, separate runs) are always appended because they may legitimately produce the same text. The renderer's existing `duplicateCount` collapse handles visual dedup for consecutive identical messages.

**Stale stream fallback prevention**: When a duplicate terminal payload is detected (final or aborted), the message is treated as fully handled. The code no longer falls through to the stream fallback, which could contain stale or different text from an earlier delta burst.

## Changed files

- `ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts` - `isTailDuplicate` helper, guards on same-run final/aborted/stream-fallback paths; cross-run path always appends; duplicate terminal payloads skip stream fallback
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts` - 7 new tests: same-run dedup, cross-run preservation, stream-fallback dedup, aborted dedup, stale-stream-on-final-duplicate, stale-stream-on-aborted-duplicate

## Verification

```
Terminal: pnpm vitest run ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts

 Ō£ō |unit-ui| ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts (98 tests) 22ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  98 passed (98)
```

Behavioral checks:
- Same-run final with matching tail -> skipped (race condition dedup)
- Same-run final duplicate + stale chatStream -> stream text NOT appended (P2 fix)
- Same-run aborted duplicate + stale chatStream -> stream text NOT appended (P2 fix)
- Cross-run final with matching tail -> appended (legitimate repeat, renderer handles display collapse)
- Stream-fallback with matching tail -> skipped (same-run race)
- Aborted message with matching tail -> skipped (same-run race)
- Different content -> always appended regardless of run

Closes #85771

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** (1) Duplicate assistant messages appear in WebChat when `loadChatHistory` and the `final` chat event race. The `isTailDuplicate` guard detects when the same message arrives via both paths. (2) When the duplicate terminal message was detected, the code previously fell through to the stream fallback. If `chatStream` contained a different partial or stale value from an earlier delta burst, that stale text was appended instead of treating the duplicate terminal as fully handled. Both the `final` and `aborted` branches had this issue.

- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node 26.0.0, OpenClaw source checkout at `/tmp/wt-86646`. UI code exercises the production `handleChatEvent` function from `ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts` via `npx tsx`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Step 1. Ran the full test suite to confirm all 98 tests pass (including 2 new stale-stream tests):

```bash
cd /tmp/wt-86646 && npx vitest run ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts
```

Step 2. Ran a proof script that imports the production `handleChatEvent` and exercises three scenarios:

```bash
cd /tmp/wt-86646 && npx tsx /tmp/proof-86646.ts
```

The proof script creates real `ChatState` objects with stale `chatStream` values and duplicate terminal payloads, then asserts the stale stream text is never appended.

- **Evidence after fix:**

Test suite output (98 tests, all passing):

```console
$ npx vitest run ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts
 Ō£ō |unit-ui| ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts (98 tests) 22ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  98 passed (98)
```

Production proof output:

```console
$ npx tsx /tmp/proof-86646.ts

=== Test 1: duplicate final with stale chatStream ===
  PASS  returns 'final'
  PASS  chatMessages length stays 1
  PASS  stale stream text NOT appended
  PASS  original message preserved

=== Test 2: duplicate aborted with stale chatStream ===
  PASS  returns 'aborted'
  PASS  chatMessages length stays 1
  PASS  stale stream text NOT appended
  PASS  original message preserved

=== Test 3: cross-run final still appends new message ===
  PASS  chatMessages length is 2
  PASS  new cross-run message appended
  PASS  active stream preserved

=== Summary: 11 passed, 0 failed ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The production `handleChatEvent` correctly handles duplicate terminal payloads without falling through to the stale stream fallback. When a final or aborted payload matches the chat tail (history-load/final-event race), the message is treated as "already visible" and skipped, with no stream text appended. When a cross-run final arrives from a different runId, it is always appended regardless of tail content. The fix restructures the conditional nesting so that `isTailDuplicate` returning true stays within the "valid terminal message" branch instead of falling through to `else if (chatStream)`.

- **What was not tested:** Live WebChat UI with a real gateway session producing the history-load/final-event race condition with a stale `chatStream` value.

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers clean up temp files, show a warning, and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Keep best-effort for non-interrupt failures**: non-SIGINT doctor failures continue with the same behavior as current main (warn but don't block dashboard)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer treats it as a regular failure and continues.

**Real environment tested**: macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash 5.x (Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw 2026.5.22 installed.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Sent SIGINT to the installer during the upgrade doctor phase on a real macOS installation with OpenClaw already installed, then verified the installer exits cleanly.

**Evidence after fix**: terminal output from the patched installer on macOS with OpenClaw installed:

```console
$ timeout --signal=INT 10 bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1; echo "EXIT=$?"

  OpenClaw Installer

Detected: macos
Install plan: macos, npm

[1/3] Preparing environment
Node.js v24.15.0 found

[2/3] Installing OpenClaw
OpenClaw installed

[3/3] Finalizing setup
OpenClaw installed successfully (2026.5.22)!
Config already present; running doctor
Running doctor to migrate settings
Running doctor

! Installation interrupted
EXIT=124
```

Verification of the SIGINT detection in run_doctor:

```console
$ grep -n 'abort_install_int\|trap.*INT\|doctor_exit.*130' scripts/install.sh
35:abort_install_int() {
47:trap abort_install_int INT
2825:    if (( doctor_exit == 130 )); then
2826:        abort_install_int
3289:            if (( doctor_exit == 130 )); then
3290:                abort_install_int
```

The INT trap at line 47 catches Ctrl+C at any point. Lines 2825-2826 and 3289-3290 specifically detect when doctor exits with 130 (SIGINT convention) and abort cleanly.

**Observed result after fix**: SIGINT during the doctor phase triggers `abort_install_int`, prints "Installation interrupted", and exits 130 (124 when wrapped by `timeout`). No dashboard opens. Non-interrupt doctor failures continue with best-effort behavior (same as current main).

**What was not tested**: Full interactive install with a real TTY Ctrl+C during a live Clack doctor prompt (requires manual TTY interaction with rendered Clack prompts). SIGTERM delivery (tested SIGINT only). Live CDN `curl | bash` path.

target.title: fix(agents): suppress EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError on user-iniŌĆ”
target.body:
## Summary

When a user presses the Stop button during agent thinking, the session fence mechanism detects session file modification and throws `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError`. This error is injected into the conversation context, polluting the agent's next turn with noise.

This PR identifies user-initiated aborts and suppresses the takeover error from conversation context while still logging it for debugging. The abort provenance fix ensures that **restart** and **auth-revoked** abort paths are NOT classified as user-initiated.

### Key Changes

- Add `shouldSuppressTakeoverErrorOnUserAbort()` helper function
- Add `isNonUserAbortReason()` helper to detect restart/auth-revoked/timeout abort reasons from the signal
- **`createChatAbortSignalReason`** now returns structured Error reasons for `restart` (AbortError) and `auth-revoked` (AbortError) in addition to `timeout` (TimeoutError)
- `userInitiatedAbort` predicate excludes non-user abort reasons via `isNonUserAbortReason`
- Log warning instead of `Promise.reject` for user abort case
- Session fence mechanism still effective (safety boundary preserved)

### Issue Reference

Fixes #88903

### Codex Review Response

Addresses **[P1] Gate suppression on explicit user-stop provenance** finding:
- Original logic treated all non-timeout `abortSignal.aborted` as user-initiated
- Gateway restart (`stopReason: "restart"`) and provider auth logout (`stopReason: "auth-revoked"`) use the same `AbortSignal.aborted` without a timeout reason
- Fix: `createChatAbortSignalReason` now returns structured reasons for restart and auth-revoked; `isNonUserAbortReason` checks these; `userInitiatedAbort` predicate excludes them
- Ensures restart and auth-revoked takeover errors remain visible as fatal errors

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior addressed**: Suppresses `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` from conversation context when user presses Stop button, preventing error pollution in agent's next turn. Timeout, restart, and auth-revoked aborts still surface takeover errors as fatal ŌĆö only genuine user Stop-button presses trigger suppression.

**Real environment tested**: Linux (Ubuntu), Node.js 22.22, `07 Open Source/02-projects/openclaw` checkout on branch `fix/suppress-session-takeover-error-on-abort-88903`, commit 7f4d2a1df.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
# 1. Run focused abort provenance helper tests (9 scenarios)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.abort-provenance.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

# 2. Run session lock lifecycle tests (42 existing tests for regression)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts --run
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.abort-provenance.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

 Ō£ō isNonUserAbortReason > returns true for TimeoutError
 Ō£ō isNonUserAbortReason > returns true for restart AbortError
 Ō£ō isNonUserAbortReason > returns true for auth-revoked AbortError
 Ō£ō isNonUserAbortReason > returns false for undefined
 Ō£ō isNonUserAbortReason > returns false for plain AbortError without restart/auth-revoked message
 Ō£ō isNonUserAbortReason > returns false for generic Error
 Ō£ō shouldSuppressTakeoverErrorOnUserAbort > returns true when userInitiatedAbort and cleanupError is takeover error
 Ō£ō shouldSuppressTakeoverErrorOnUserAbort > returns false when not userInitiatedAbort
 Ō£ō shouldSuppressTakeoverErrorOnUserAbort > returns false when cleanupError is not takeover error

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts --run

 Ō£ō |agents| src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts (42 tests) 432ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  42 passed (42)
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- User Stop (plain AbortError, no restart/auth-revoked message) + Takeover Error: suppressed from conversation (log.warn only), agent next turn stays clean Ō£ō
- Timeout + Takeover Error: NOT suppressed (isNonUserAbortReason returns true for TimeoutError) Ō£ō
- Restart + Takeover Error: NOT suppressed (isNonUserAbortReason returns true for restart AbortError) Ō£ō
- Auth-revoked + Takeover Error: NOT suppressed (isNonUserAbortReason returns true for auth-revoked AbortError) Ō£ō
- User Stop + Other Error: NOT suppressed (not a takeover error) Ō£ō
- Session fence detection remains active for all abort types Ō£ō
- All 51 tests pass (42 session-lock + 9 abort-provenance) with no regressions Ō£ō

**What was not tested**: Live UI Stop button press in actual OpenClaw dashboard (requires running dev gateway), full agent runner workflow with real provider/model calls, multi-agent concurrent access scenarios.

---

**Related issues**: #87966, #88369, #88457, #88471, #88653 (distinct from this fix - those address false positives, this addresses UX impact of legitimate user abort)

target.title: fix(outbound): keep channel send durable when transcript mirror fails (#89626)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #89626. The post-delivery transcript **mirror** in `deliverOutboundPayloads`
runs *after* the channel payloads are already sent. Its append was unguarded, so a
transient session-lock failure (`session file changed while embedded prompt lock was
released`) propagated out of the send. The whole send was then reported as `failed`
ŌĆö discarding the fact that the channel message had already been delivered ŌĆö and the
sub-agent announce layer retried, re-delivering the message up to **3 times** before
giving up.

The mirror is best-effort local bookkeeping. This change catches both thrown errors
and `{ ok: false }` results, logs a warning, and lets the already-successful channel
delivery stand. This matches the existing cron delivery path
(`mirrorDirectDeliveryIntoTranscript` in `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.ts`),
so it is consistent with the sibling surface rather than a one-sided patch. The append
still carries its idempotency key, so any later reconciliation stays deduplicated.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: duplicate sub-agent completion announcement (delivered up to 3x) when the post-send transcript mirror fails on the session lock.

Real environment tested: built OpenClaw source on macOS (Node 22.22.3), exercising the real `deliverOutboundPayloads` runtime path with a real channel outbound adapter and the real subsystem logger. The documented `session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released` lock contention was injected at the transcript-mirror append (env `OPENCLAW_FAULT_MIRROR_THROW=1`) so the race reproduces deterministically; everything downstream is the unmodified production delivery pipeline.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: drove the production `deliverOutboundPayloads({ channel, payloads, mirror })` once with the mirror append forced to fail, observing the channel adapter call count and the runtime log. Ran the same path against the pre-fix (unguarded) and post-fix code.

Evidence after fix (copied runtime/terminal output):

```text
# pre-fix (unguarded mirror append) + injected lock failure
[channel] matrix delivered to !session:main: "Sub-agent run b8efbcc9 complete: task finished."
[repro] deliverOutboundPayloads THREW: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released
[repro] channel send count = 1 (message already on the wire)

# post-fix (this patch) + injected lock failure
[channel] matrix delivered to !session:main: "Sub-agent run b8efbcc9 complete: task finished."
[outbound/deliver] failed to mirror outbound delivery into session transcript; channel send already succeeded: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released
[repro] deliverOutboundPayloads returned 1 result(s); did NOT throw.
[repro] channel send count = 1
```

Observed result after fix: with the lock failure injected, the channel adapter is invoked exactly once and `deliverOutboundPayloads` returns the delivered result instead of throwing; the mirror failure is downgraded to a single `[outbound/deliver]` warning. Pre-fix, the same condition throws after the message is already on the wire, which is what drives the sub-agent announce retry to re-deliver duplicates.

What was not tested: an end-to-end live Telegram + Raspberry Pi sub-agent run (no such hardware/model here). The session-lock race was injected deterministically at the mirror-append boundary rather than reproduced via natural timing.

## Supplemental checks (not a substitute for the proof above)

- `pnpm test src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts` ŌĆö 95/95 pass, including 2 new regression tests that fail without this patch.
- `pnpm test` on siblings (`message`, `outbound-send-service`, `subagent-announce-delivery`) ŌĆö 126/126 pass.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` + `pnpm tsgo:test:src` ŌĆö exit 0; `oxfmt --check` + oxlint clean.

## Files
- `src/infra/outbound/deliver.ts` ŌĆö guard the post-delivery transcript mirror append
- `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts` ŌĆö 2 regression tests + tighten the transcript mock type

target.title: [codex] Keep Codex waiting after raw reasoning progress
target.body:
## Summary
- Treat raw reasoning completion after a tool handoff as progress instead of a short completion-idle stall signal.
- Reuse the existing post-progress reply guard so long-running reasoning can continue until Codex sends a real terminal turn notification.
- Update the Codex turn-watch regression test to reproduce the stale-session sequence and prove it survives past the short guard.

## Root cause
A chilisaus task stalled because the embedded Codex app-server retired the turn after 60s of no stream events. Loki showed the last activity was rawResponseItem/completed with item type reasoning. The current OpenClaw logic armed the short completion-idle watch for that raw reasoning completion, so reasoning silence could be misclassified as a terminal stall.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Long-running Codex/chilisaus turns could be retired after 60 seconds when the last app-server notification was raw reasoning progress, leaving the delivery watcher alive but the underlying agent session stalled.
- Real environment tested: Alex's mac-mini OpenClaw production setup, Telegram-triggered chilisaus dispatch, Loki/OTel logs, and the local OpenClaw source checkout on this PR branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Queried Loki/OTel for the failed production run, observed the scheduler-side watchdog fail the stale run after the watcher fix, then ran the patched OpenClaw Codex turn-watch path locally with the focused command shown under validation.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live output from the real OpenClaw Telegram/scheduler run after the watchdog safety net was active:

  ```text
  Scheduler | chilisaus-deliver:build-soccer-betting-model | 59s ago
  ŌØī chilisaus [build-soccer-betting-model] failed
  Error: agent session stalled: no session/jsonl activity for ~77min while delivery watcher remained alive; likely app-server turn retired or stopped producing events
  ```

  Redacted Loki/OTel trace excerpt from the same real incident before this OpenClaw patch identified the app-server root cause:

  ```text
  2026-06-01 20:08:46 EDT codex app-server turn idle timed out waiting for completion
  idleMs=60000 timeoutMs=60000
  lastActivityReason=notification:rawResponseItem/completed
  lastNotificationMethod=rawResponseItem/completed
  lastNotificationItemType=reasoning
  reason=turn_completion_idle_timeout
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The scheduler no longer silently reports the stale job as running; it surfaces a hard stalled-session failure. This PR addresses the upstream OpenClaw side by keeping raw reasoning progress on the longer post-progress guard instead of the short 60s completion-idle path that caused that Loki trace.
- What was not tested: The patched OpenClaw source was not deployed/restarted on the production gateway in this PR run.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The production gateway was not restarted from this session, so the live runtime proof shows the real incident and safety-net behavior; the OpenClaw app-server behavior change is validated in the focused local regression below.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The Loki/OTel trace excerpt above is the before evidence for the app-server root cause.

## Validation
- pnpm test -- extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts
- Result: 1 test file passed, 55 tests passed

target.title: fix(gateway): fail fast on missing local probe auth
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a fail-fast in `openclaw gateway status` for the local loopback case: when `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials but neither the persisted token nor an interactive credential can be resolved, the status command synthesises a failed probe with a clear `failureReason` instead of attempting an anonymous WebSocket connection that surfaces as a misleading `Connect: failed`.

Explicit `--url`/`--rpc-url` targets are intentionally exempt: a user pointing at a remote, forwarded, or open gateway should not be blocked by missing local-config credentials.

## Details

- `resolveAuthForTarget` resolves through the probe-surface helper. When the result has neither token nor password AND the target is the known local loopback AND `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials, it falls back to the interactive-surface helper and propagates the failure reason.
- The fail-fast is scoped to `surface === "local" && target.kind === "localLoopback"`. Targets with `kind === "explicit"` (i.e. `--url`/`--rpc-url`) skip the check.
- Tests cover both the local fail-fast path and the explicit-target carve-out, and isolate ambient `gateway.auth.token` env in the probe-auth test to avoid cross-test contamination.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: before this patch, `openclaw gateway status` against a local config with `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` but an unresolvable token (e.g. `secrets.token.env` points at a non-existent env var) would still try an anonymous WebSocket connection and surface `Connect: failed` to the operator. The operator-actionable diagnosis ("missing required local auth") was buried under a misleading network error. After this patch, the probe is short-circuited with a `failureReason` that names the auth shape.

- **Real environment tested**: deployed `openclaw` runtime built from this PR head on a local laptop checkout at `/tmp/wt-68280` (rebased onto `upstream/main` at `8e1b0e61b0`), run against an isolated test config at `/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json` so the live mac-mini gateway and its real auth are not touched. The test config sets `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` and `gateway.secrets.token.env` to an env var that is explicitly unset in the shell.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  unset OPENCLAW_PROBE_NONEXISTENT_TEST_VAR
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from the patched `node dist/index.js` runtime built from this PR head. The first scenario is the local-loopback fail-fast; the second is the explicit-`--url` carve-out.

  ```
  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Missing gateway auth token.",
    "kind": null
  }

  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  {
    "rpc": {
      "ok": false,
      "error": "connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:18999",
      "kind": "connect"
    },
    "probeUrl": "ws://127.0.0.1:18999"
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: in the local-loopback scenario, `rpc.kind` is `null` (no probe attempt was made) and `rpc.error` names the actionable auth diagnostic instead of a network error. In the explicit-URL scenario, `rpc.kind` is `"connect"` (the probe DID run) and the error is a real network-level `ECONNREFUSED` against the URL the operator supplied. The design boundary holds: missing local SecretRef diagnostics do not leak into the explicit-URL probe contract.

- **What was not tested**: the explicit-URL daemon `--rpc-url` flag was not exercised end-to-end in this proof. The carve-out is structural (`target.kind !== "explicit"` is the only gate) and is covered by the regression tests added in `c6c648a9cd` and `8e1b0e61b0`.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` ŌåÆ `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner ŌĆö the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function ŌĆö bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function ŌĆö pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check ŌĆö has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key ŌĆö so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` ŌåÆ `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` ŌĆö 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits ŌĆö the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) ŌĆö the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps ŌĆö (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` ŌåÆ stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable ŌĆö this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None ŌĆö this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** ŌĆö the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token ŌåÆ mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   ŌåÆ mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity ŌĆö it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
